body_txt: This is when the Fu Manchu mustache is used to evoke and reference Asia , martial arts, and military traditions. Despite being named after Fu Manchu and tied to one of the most infamous racist caricatures of the 20th century, the mustache continues to be used to evoke Asian identity and cultures. Characters with the Fu Manchu mustache are often distanced from the eponymous figure. Fu Manchu was an assassin , crime boss, Mad Scientist , and a Supervillain who sought to destroy Western civilization, relying on his minions to carry out his schemes while seldom engaging in confrontation note he used agents, chemicals, enforcers, mind control, wild animals, and diseases . Because of Fu Manchu’s controversy, creators often give characters virtuous qualities and emphasize martial arts . The mustache is prominent in Wuxia and references various Asian military cultures, particularly the Hun and Mongolian Empires. The Fu Manchu mustache is worn by fighters, martial artists, and warriors. Characters with the Fu Manchu mustache are often portrayed with an aura of enigma and wisdom. They are frequently depicted as the Bare-Fisted Monk , Kung-Fu Wizard , or Warrior Monk . They typically possess special techniques or Supernatural Martial Arts . When martial artists wear this mustache , it signifies their authority, and their power shouldn't be underestimated . The length of the mustache often reflects how long they have been training and fighting . Characters with the Fu Manchu mustache are also portrayed as cunning and deadly warriors note Samurai , Ninja , Samurai Shinobi , Street Samurai , Samurai Cowboy , etc. The mustache often suggests inspiration from figures like Attila the Hun and Genghis Khan . Despite their fearsome reputations, the mustache can also evoke the positive aspects of both empires. The Huns and the Mongols became two of the most powerful forces in history by uniting various nomadic tribes and training them into highly efficient military units. Under Genghis Khan’s leadership, the Mongols were so formidable that they established the “Pax Mongolica,” a period of relative stability across Eurasia during the 13th and 14th centuries , supported by his system of meritocracy. Villains may exhibit attributes and physical characteristics reminiscent of Attila the Hun or Genghis Khan to highlight their infamy and negative traits. Such villains might possess the Fatal Flaw of being short-sighted , narrow-minded , and indifferent to collateral damage because they lack the patience to think through their actions or recognize how taking shortcuts can become more harmful and destructive to others. Examples include the “Mongol Conquest of Western Xia” note where the Mongols attempted to divert a river through Yinchuan to flood the city, but the plan backfired, flooding their own camps instead. . It is also speculated that when the Mongols catapulted plague-infected corpses into Caffa during a 14th-century siege to weaken enemy forces, the act may have had the unforeseen consequence of contributing to the spread of the Black Death note blaming the Mongols entirely for the plague would overlook several other contributing factors. Biological warfare was not unique to the Mongol Empire, as other empires were also known to have used similar tactics. There were also religious biases and widespread misinformation surrounding the plague; for example, Scotland was infected with the plague after attempting to exploit the outbreaks in England for an invasion, only for the effort to fail and spread the infection further, as the invaders believed it affected only the English and was a sign from God. Another major factor was that the 14th century had poor food safety practices and extremely low health and hygiene standards, which likely prolonged the pandemic and contributed to additional outbreaks. Rats and fleas also carried the disease and helped spread it across regions. The Mongols were accused of causing the plague largely because of their empire’s extensive trade routes and corresponding patterns of migration . This should be used cautiously to avoid parallels to the COVID-19 pandemic. The mustache serves as a visual cue for Asian-inspired culture and themes. Its use signals that the creator wants their characters to be perceived as having an Asian identity, suggesting that their worlds and characters have been shaped by Asian culture, people, and history. In stories, elements such as Anachronism Stew , Culture Chop Suey , Fantasy Counterpart Culture , and Interchangeable Asian Cultures often appear in fantasy settings. Anthropomorphic characters may sport a Fu Manchu mustache or feature an alternative attribute that evokes their appearance, such as Animal Facial Hair , a Clothing Appendage , a Hair Substitute Feature , or a Weird Beard . Western creators have often relied on Refuge in Audacity , claiming that a non-Asian character’s Fu Manchu mustache appears in an in-universe dream sequence, an imaginary scenario, or an elaborate homage to the martial arts genre. However, the general consensus is that such roles should be portrayed authentically and respectfully by Asian actors to avoid ethnic stereotyping and insensitive humor. The Fu Manchu mustache is irreparably associated with Yellowface , due to its connection with Fu Manchu. For this reason, they are treated as separate tropes. The Fu Manchu mustache is a physical trait, whereas Yellowface is a performance that relies on makeup and costumes. Historically, Yellowface was used to entertain, deceive, or humiliate, until it was eventually recognized as a racist caricature. As long as the Fu Manchu mustache does not reinforce negative stereotypes or form part of a caricature, it is considered acceptable to wear, unlike yellowface . This should not be confused with the horseshoe mustache . The Fu Manchu mustache is thinner than the horseshoe, grows only from the corners of the upper lip, and hangs past the chin. Some variants of the Fu Manchu resemble a thin horseshoe or feature a shaved upper lip with hair left to grow solely at the corners. In contrast, the horseshoe mustache is thicker, features two vertical columns of hair that extend down to the chin, and forms an upside-down “U” shape. They are separated because the Fu Manchu carries distinct cultural baggage and a problematic history. This trope is not to be confused with Faux Manchu , a villainous Asian stock character from the Yellow Peril era whose design was codified by Fu Manchu. Despite the name, not all characters with a Fu Manchu mustache are based on the eponymous character, but they can overlap. This trope goes with Manly Facial Hair and Good Hair, Evil Hair . It can also go with Inscrutable Oriental . See also Godwin's Law of Facial Hair for another style of facial hair that is overshadowed by controversy and used cautiously. Can go with 'Stache of Duty if the character is part of a military organisation.
